name: Electrochemistry
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Galvanic (Voltaic) Cells
sections.body:
A galvanic cell converts the chemical energy of a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy. It has two half-cells: an oxidation half (anode, −) and a reduction half (cathode, +), connected externally by a wire and internally by a salt bridge that maintains electrical neutrality.

Daniell cell: Zn(s) | Zn²⁺(aq) || Cu²⁺(aq) | Cu(s). At the anode: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; at the cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. Cell reaction: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu.

Cell potential Ecell = Ecathode − Eanode (both as reduction potentials). When Ecell > 0 the reaction is spontaneous.

sections.heading: Standard Electrode Potential and SHE
sections.body:
The absolute potential of a single electrode cannot be measured; we measure relative to the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE): Pt | H2(g, 1 bar) | H⁺(aq, 1 M), assigned E° = 0 V.

The standard electrode potential E° is the potential when all species are at unit activity (1 M, 1 bar, 298 K). The series of E° values is the electrochemical series. More negative E° → stronger reducing agent (Li, K, Ca at top); more positive E° → stronger oxidising agent (F2, Au³⁺ at bottom).

Predicting reactions: a metal can displace another metal from its salt only if its E° is more negative (lower in the series).

sections.heading: Nernst Equation
sections.body:
The Nernst equation gives cell potential at non-standard conditions:

For an electrode Mn+ + n e⁻ → M:
E = E° − (RT / nF) · ln(1/[Mn+]) = E° + (0.0591/n) · log[Mn+] at 298 K.

For a complete cell: Ecell = E°cell − (0.0591/n) · log Q, where Q is the reaction quotient.

At equilibrium, Ecell = 0 and Q = Kc: E°cell = (0.0591/n) · log Kc.

Relation to free energy: ΔG = −nFE; ΔG° = −nFE°cell = −RT ln Kc.

sections.heading: Conductance of Electrolytic Solutions
sections.body:
Conductance (G) = 1/R, unit siemens (S). Conductivity (κ) = 1/ρ = G·(l/A), unit S m⁻¹. The factor (l/A) is the cell constant, found by calibration with a standard KCl solution.

Molar conductivity Λm = κ/c (with c in mol m⁻³, Λm in S m² mol⁻¹) or = κ × 1000/c (with c in mol L⁻¹).

Variation with concentration:

  • Strong electrolytes (KCl, HCl): Λm rises slowly as c → 0. Debye–Hückel–Onsager equation: Λm = Λm° − A·√c.
  • Weak electrolytes (CH3COOH, NH4OH): Λm rises sharply at low c due to increased dissociation.

Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions: Λm° = ν+·λ°+ + ν−·λ°−. Used to find Λm° of weak electrolytes (e.g. Λm°(CH3COOH) = Λm°(CH3COONa) + Λm°(HCl) − Λm°(NaCl)).

Degree of dissociation α = Λm/Λm°. Dissociation constant Ka = c·α²/(1 − α).

sections.heading: Electrolytic Cells and Faraday's Laws
sections.body:
An electrolytic cell uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction. Cathode is negative (where cations are reduced); anode is positive (where anions or the electrode are oxidised).

Faraday's First Law: mass deposited m ∝ Q (charge passed): m = Z·I·t, where Z is the electrochemical equivalent.

Faraday's Second Law: when same Q passes through different electrolytes, masses deposited are in ratio of their equivalent masses.

1 Faraday = 96485 C mol⁻¹ = charge of 1 mole of electrons. To deposit 1 mole of Mn+ requires n F of charge.

Products of electrolysis depend on the species present and their reduction/oxidation potentials, plus overpotential. Aqueous NaCl gives H2 at cathode and Cl2 at anode (despite O2 having lower E°, due to overpotential).

sections.heading: Batteries — Primary and Secondary
sections.body:
Primary batteries (irreversible, used once):

  • Dry cell (Leclanché): Zn anode, MnO2+C cathode in NH4Cl/ZnCl2 paste. ~1.5 V. Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; MnO2 + NH4⁺ + e⁻ → MnO(OH) + NH3.
  • Mercury cell: Zn–Hg amalgam anode, HgO+C cathode in KOH paste. Steady ~1.35 V (used in hearing aids, watches).

Secondary batteries (rechargeable):

  • Lead storage battery (car battery): Pb anode, PbO2 cathode in 38% H2SO4. Discharge: Pb + PbO2 + 2H2SO4 → 2PbSO4 + 2H2O. Recharge reverses this.
  • Nickel–Cadmium cell: Cd anode, NiO(OH) cathode. Longer life than lead cell but more expensive.

sections.heading: Fuel Cells and Corrosion
sections.body:
Fuel cells convert fuel + O2 directly to electricity. Hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell (Apollo missions): porous C electrodes with Pt/Pd catalyst in KOH electrolyte. Anode: 2H2 + 4OH⁻ → 4H2O + 4e⁻. Cathode: O2 + 2H2O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻. Overall: 2H2 + O2 → 2H2O. ~70% efficient, pollution-free.

Corrosion is electrochemical oxidation of metals. Iron rusting: at anodic site, Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ (E° = −0.44 V); at cathodic site, O2 + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H2O. Fe²⁺ then oxidises further to Fe2O3·xH2O (rust).

Prevention: barrier methods (paint, oil, plating with Sn or Cr); cathodic protection (sacrificial Mg or Zn anode that corrodes preferentially); galvanisation (Zn coating).

summary: Electrochemistry connects redox chemistry with electricity. Galvanic cells exploit spontaneous reactions: E_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode and ΔG = −nFE. The Nernst equation, E = E° − (0.0591/n)log Q at 298 K, links potential to concentration; at equilibrium it gives K_c. Conductivity κ and molar conductivity Λ_m describe ion transport; Kohlrausch's law allows Λ_m° of weak electrolytes to be computed from limiting ionic conductivities. Electrolysis follows Faraday's laws (1 F = 96485 C deposits 1 equivalent). Practical applications include dry, mercury, lead-storage, Ni-Cd batteries, H_2–O_2 fuel cells, and corrosion control via cathodic protection.
pitfalls: Forgetting that E°_cell uses both as reduction potentials (do not flip sign of anode and add).
Using log instead of ln in Nernst — the factor 0.0591 already absorbs (RT/F)·ln10 at 298 K.
Mixing up moles of electrons n in Nernst (per mole of reaction as balanced).
Forgetting overpotential — predicted product (e.g. O_2 at anode) may not be the actual product (Cl_2 in conc NaCl).
Confusing cathode/anode polarity: in galvanic cell anode is −; in electrolytic cell anode is +.
Using c in mol/L without converting in Λ_m = κ × 1000/c (when κ is in S/cm).
Forgetting to take overall n_electrons across whole reaction in Faraday calculations.
